We are told that a total of ninety-six students live in both buildings combined.

Let us define the initial number of students in Oakwood as a variable.
Let us also define the initial number of students in Maplewood as another variable.
The sum of these two variables must equal the total of ninety-six.
After the transfer, the new population in Oakwood is the original plus five.
The new population in Maplewood is the original minus five.
These two new populations are stated to be exactly equal to each other.
We can set up an equation where the new Oakwood count equals the new Maplewood count.
Solving this equation reveals the relationship between the starting numbers.
The difference between the starting numbers must have been exactly ten students.
Maplewood must have started with more students than Oakwood did.

If we divide the total of ninety-six by two, we get the equalized number of forty-eight.
This means after the move, each dormitory held forty-eight students.
To find the original number in Oakwood, we must subtract the five who arrived.
Forty-eight minus five equals forty-three students originally in Oakwood.
To find the original number in Maplewood, we must add the five who left.
Forty-eight plus five equals fifty-three students originally in Maplewood.
Checking the math confirms that forty-three plus fifty-three equals ninety-six.
The move of five students from fifty-three leaves forty-eight in Maplewood.
Adding those five to forty-three brings Oakwood up to forty-eight as well.
The condition of equal populations after the move is perfectly satisfied.
The question asks for the number of students in Oakwood before the relocation.
Our calculation shows that number to be exactly forty-three.
